features.pitch: First proxy with a native MCP server: agents get scoped, guardrailed, auto-rollback SQL sessions with per-agent token budgets and a tamper-evident audit trail — turning the existing AI plugin suite into a product category.
features.rationale: The market is moving here — Odyssey 1.4 now markets itself as an 'AI-ready... request router', and Postgres MCP access is the fastest-growing agent-database pattern of 2025-26 — but no pooler actually enforces anything about agent traffic. HeliosProxy already owns the enforcement layer: ai-classifier (extracts agent_id/model_id), token-budget (per-agent cost gating), llm-guardrail (refuses dangerous AI SQL), audit-chain (hash-chained log), anomaly detection, and rate limiting are all shipped, signed first-party plugins. What's missing is the agent-facing surface: an MCP endpoint on the admin port exposing query/topology/analytics tools, plus sandboxed sessions (every agent session wrapped in a transaction that auto-rolls-back unless explicitly committed through a guardrail check — buildable on ha-tr session state machinery and the transaction journal). The HeliosDB-CodeKB-MCP companion proves the org already has MCP DNA. Nobody — not RDS Proxy, not Neon, not pgDog — can bundle classification, budgeting, guardrails, audit, and sandboxing for agents at the wire level today.
features.buildsOn: first-party plugins (ai-classifier, token-budget, llm-guardrail, audit-chain) via wasm-plugins, src/auth/api_keys.rs, ha-tr session migration/transaction journal, admin REST stack, anomaly-detection

features.name: MCP Gateway (`mcp-gateway` feature)
features.pitch: Native MCP server on the proxy: agents call query/schema/explain tools over MCP, and every call inherits the full plugin policy chain.
features.rationale: In 2026 agents speak MCP first, PG wire second — yet the proxy, which already holds auth, quotas, guardrails, and routing, has no MCP surface; the ecosystem ships MCP only in HeliosDB-Nano and the CodeKB-MCP repo, leaving the data plane unaddressable by Claude/Codex/agent frameworks without a driver and raw connection string. A `tools/query`, `tools/list_schema`, `tools/explain` MCP endpoint on the admin port (the hand-rolled HTTP server in src/admin.rs already serves JSON + an embedded UI, so SSE/streamable-HTTP is incremental) would make HeliosProxy the obvious front door: each MCP session maps to a pooled backend session, and every tool call flows through the existing pre-query hooks so ai-classifier, token-budget, llm-guardrail, and cost-governor gate MCP traffic with zero new policy code. No competing PG pooler (PgBouncer, PgCat, Supavisor) offers this; it is the single highest-differentiation move available.
features.buildsOn: src/admin.rs HTTP surface, src/auth/ (api_keys.rs, jwt.rs) for MCP session auth, src/pool/ + src/backend/client.rs for session-mapped execution, src/plugins/ hook chain (hook_context already carries agent_id/model_id), src/analytics for per-tool metrics

features.name: Per-Agent Identities with Scoped Grants
features.pitch: Proxy-minted, short-lived agent credentials whose table/column/operation scopes are enforced at the wire level — agent IAM without touching PG roles.
features.rationale: Today every agent run shares the app's DB user; blast radius is the whole schema and audit attribution is by application_name string-matching (the ai-classifier plugin's heuristic). The market gap: no PG proxy issues ephemeral per-run credentials with a declarative grant manifest (tables, columns, verbs, row filters, max rows) compiled into a statement-level allowlist checked before forwarding. The pieces exist: src/auth/role_mapper.rs maps external identity to roles, src/auth/api_keys.rs issues keys, src/multi_tenancy/identifier.rs resolves identity from connection metadata, and src/rewriter/ can inject mandatory row filters (WHERE tenant_id = ...) — what's missing is the grant-manifest store (plugin KV or proxy.toml), TTL-bound credential minting, and the enforcement interceptor. Also fixes a latent cache-safety issue: keying the query cache by grant scope prevents a low-privilege agent's miss being served a higher-privilege agent's cached rows.
features.buildsOn: src/auth/role_mapper.rs + api_keys.rs, src/multi_tenancy/identifier.rs, src/rewriter/ (filter injection), ai-classifier's agent_id extraction in hook_context, query-cache key derivation

features.name: Query-Cost Preflight (dry-run API for agents)
features.pitch: Agents ask 'what will this query cost?' before running it; the proxy answers from EXPLAIN-on-replica plus its own fingerprint history, and gates expensive queries behind a confirm token.
features.rationale: Agents routinely emit accidental O(n^2) joins and unbounded scans; the existing llm-guardrail only catches syntactic red flags (missing LIMIT/WHERE), not a 40M-row hash join with a LIMIT on top. The proxy is uniquely placed to predict: src/rate_limit/cost_estimator.rs already does heuristic costing, src/analytics/histogram.rs + statistics.rs hold real latency distributions per query fingerprint, and src/backend/client.rs can run EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON) against a replica without touching the primary. Expose POST /api/preflight (and an MCP `explain` tool): returns predicted cost, est. rows, lock risk, and historical p95 for the fingerprint; above a per-agent threshold the query requires a confirm token, turning runaway-query prevention into an agent-legible protocol. This converts three existing modules into a feature no pooler has, at moderate effort.
features.buildsOn: src/rate_limit/cost_estimator.rs, src/analytics/{histogram,statistics,fingerprinter}.rs, src/backend/client.rs (EXPLAIN execution), routing (replica selection), token-budget plugin for threshold config via plugin KV

features.name: Sandbox Branch Sessions for Agent Experimentation
features.pitch: An agent connection flagged `sandbox=true` gets its writes routed to a copy-on-write branch; the proxy journals everything and offers diff, discard, or replay-to-promote.
features.rationale: The number-one ask for agentic DB workloads is 'let the agent try the migration/backfill without touching prod' — Neon built a company on branching, but it requires their storage layer; HeliosProxy can deliver it at the proxy layer against vanilla PG: route the session's writes to a clone (CREATE DATABASE ... TEMPLATE, or HeliosDB-Nano's native branching), capture every statement in the transaction journal, then either discard or replay the journal window onto the real primary via the existing replay engine. Nearly every hard component already exists in-tree: src/transaction_journal.rs (statement-level capture with savepoints), src/replay/ (journal-window replay with credential overrides, shipped as POST /api/replay), src/shadow_execute/ (order-independent row-hash diffing for branch-vs-prod comparison), and src/upgrade_orchestrator/ (the exact stand-up/validate/cutover state-machine pattern, including SwitchoverBuffer integration). The XL effort is clone provisioning and promote-conflict semantics; the payoff is the marquee demo for agent safety.
features.buildsOn: src/transaction_journal.rs, src/replay/ (time-travel replay), src/shadow_execute/ (diff), src/upgrade_orchestrator/ (state machine + SwitchoverBuffer), src/schema_routing/ for branch routing

features.name: SQL Contract Validator with Machine-Readable Repair Hints
features.pitch: Blocked AI queries return structured JSON repair hints ('missing tenant filter: add WHERE tenant_id = $1') instead of opaque errors, so agents self-correct in one round trip.
features.rationale: llm-guardrail already blocks dangerous AI SQL, but a bare PG ErrorResponse is a dead end for an agent — it retries blindly or gives up; the 10x is making every policy verdict agent-legible. Define per-agent contract manifests (required predicates, allowed verbs, mandatory LIMIT, parameterization rules) stored in plugin KV, validate generated SQL against them, and when blocking, emit a structured hint in the error's DETAIL field (and as a typed MCP error): violation class, offending clause, suggested rewrite template. This also subsumes NL2SQL-with-policy: whether SQL comes from the agent or a future synthesis endpoint, the contract layer is the enforcement point, and the repair loop is what makes LLM-generated SQL converge instead of flail. Builds directly on the RouteResult::Block ABI shipped in v0.4.0 (which already synthesizes ErrorResponse + ReadyForQuery) — extending Block with a structured payload is a small ABI addition with outsized agent-UX impact.
features.buildsOn: llm-guardrail plugin, RouteResult::Block ABI (src/plugins/runtime.rs), plugin KV (src/plugins/host_imports.rs), src/analytics/intent.rs for verb/intent classification, src/rewriter/ for suggested-rewrite templates

features.name: Agent Flight Recorder (per-run audit timeline)
features.pitch: Every agent run gets a queryable, hash-chained timeline — each query with fingerprint, cost, rows touched, plugin verdicts — exported as OTel spans keyed by agent_id/run_id.
features.rationale: EU AI Act-era compliance and plain debugging both demand 'show me exactly what the agent did to the database' — and the proxy sees all of it but currently scatters the evidence across audit-chain entries, analytics stats, and anomaly events with no per-run join key. The feature: propagate run/trace identity via connection params or traceparent SQL comments, correlate every query + plugin verdict + result size under (agent_id, run_id), serve GET /agents/{id}/runs/{run}/trace from the admin API, and emit OTel spans (the opentelemetry dep is already in Cargo.toml behind the observability feature, currently underused). The hash-chained tamper evidence comes free from the audit-chain plugin's env.sha256_hex pattern. This is the cheapest of the eight relative to its sales impact: 'agent audit trail' is a checkbox enterprise buyers now ask for by name, and competitors have nothing at the DB-gateway layer.
features.buildsOn: audit-chain plugin + env.sha256_hex host import, src/analytics/fingerprinter.rs, src/anomaly/ event stream, observability feature (opentelemetry 0.21 dep), src/admin.rs REST surface, ai-classifier agent_id/model_id extraction

features.name: Result-Set PII Egress Guard
features.pitch: Wire-level DLP on DataRow messages flowing back to agents: detect, redact, or block PII in results regardless of how the query was phrased, and alarm on bulk-extraction patterns.
features.rationale: column-mask rewrites queries inbound (SELECT ssn -> mask_ssn(ssn)), which any view, join alias, json_agg, or function indirection bypasses — and training-data extraction is exactly the adversarial access pattern that finds those bypasses. The proxy's hand-rolled backend does text-format I/O, which makes scanning outbound DataRow messages tractable: regex + Luhn/format validators (emails, SSNs, cards, API keys) over result fields, with per-agent egress policy (redact / block / log) and a rows-per-window egress meter that flags an agent slurping the users table — wiring straight into the anomaly event stream as a new 'bulk-egress' family. No PG proxy on the market does result-side DLP; combined with column-mask this gives defense-in-depth that compliance teams can sign off on for agent access to production data. Effort is L not XL because protocol.rs already parses message framing and the regex/once_cell deps are in place; cost is a hot-path scan that should be policy-scoped to flagged AI sessions.
features.buildsOn: column-mask plugin (inbound complement), src/protocol.rs + src/backend/stream.rs (text-format DataRow parsing), src/anomaly/ (new egress-volume detector family alongside rate-spike), ai-classifier session flagging, regex/once_cell deps

features.name: Vector Workload Optimizer
features.pitch: 10x pgvector-router: adaptive ef_search/probes tuning, similarity-aware reuse of top-K results via the existing L3 cache, and ANN-vs-exact routing to tagged replicas.
features.rationale: RAG traffic is the dominant agent read pattern and pgvector-router currently only picks a replica; the proxy can do real optimization it is uniquely positioned for: (1) inject SET hnsw.ef_search / ivfflat.probes per query via the rewriter, tuned from observed latency/recall per fingerprint in analytics; (2) reuse top-K results for near-duplicate embedding literals through the L3 semantic cache (src/cache/l3_semantic.rs already does cosine-similarity matching with an Ollama embedding client — pointing it at the query's own embedding literal makes similarity caching free and answers the 'semantic caching for LLM queries' demand without a new tier); (3) classify ANN top-K vs exact/filtered scans via schema_routing's existing vector workload type and route each to appropriately tagged nodes. Moderate effort because all three substrates exist; leverage is real but narrower than the identity/safety features since it monetizes performance rather than unlocking adoption.
features.buildsOn: pgvector-router plugin, src/cache/l3_semantic.rs (similarity matching + embedding client), src/schema_routing/ (vector workload classification), src/rewriter/ (SET injection), src/analytics/histogram.rs (latency feedback loop)

features.name: Client-Facing TLS, mTLS, and SPIFFE Workload Identity (+ FIPS posture)
features.pitch: Terminate TLS from clients, authenticate apps by mTLS/SPIFFE SVID instead of passwords, and offer a FIPS-capable crypto build — the table-stakes gate for bank and healthcare procurement.
features.buildsOn: src/backend/tls.rs (rustls/tokio-rustls already a dep), src/server.rs startup handshake (currently answers SSLRequest with 'N' at server.rs:905-910), src/auth/ role_mapper + credentials, multi-tenancy tenant identification
features.rationale: Today the proxy literally rejects client SSL: server.rs sends 'N' to every SSLRequest, so no regulated workload can put it in the data path — every other feature is moot for that buyer. All the machinery exists (rustls ServerConfig is one struct away from the client codec; auth/role_mapper.rs can map cert SAN/SPIFFE ID to PG role and tenant the same way it maps JWT claims). Add cert hot-rotation via the existing plugin hot-reload watcher pattern, and a FIPS build flag swapping ring for aws-lc-rs-fips. This converts HeliosProxy from 'cool internal tool' to 'allowed past the security review', and is also the prerequisite for SPIFFE-native zero-trust meshes where PgBouncer/pgcat are weak.

features.name: Zero-Downtime Reload: SIGHUP Config Apply + SO_REUSEPORT Binary Handoff
features.pitch: Change pools, nodes, limits, and even the binary version without dropping a single client connection.
features.buildsOn: src/server.rs listener (plain TcpListener::bind at :486), src/config.rs, src/switchover_buffer.rs (already pauses/drains traffic), src/session_migrate.rs (already serializes full session state), plugins/hot_reload.rs (file-watch pattern to generalize)
features.rationale: Restart-to-reconfigure is the #1 operational objection to standardizing on any proxy; PgBouncer's RELOAD and Envoy's hot restart set the expectation. HeliosProxy uniquely already owns the two hard parts: SwitchoverBuffer holds queries mid-flight and SessionMigrate captures/restores SET state + prepared statements — so a new process can adopt sessions, not just sockets. Implementation: diff-and-apply on SIGHUP for pool/node/limit config (most fields are behind Arc<RwLock> already), and socket2-based SO_REUSEPORT dual-bind with old-process drain (the SIGTERM drain path exists) for binary upgrades. Also fixes the awkward story where WASM plugins hot-reload but proxy.toml does not.

features.name: Per-Query Distributed Tracing with traceparent Propagation into PostgreSQL
features.pitch: W3C traceparent flows from the app through the proxy into PG as a SQL comment + application_name, with a proxy span per query showing pool wait, cache tier, route decision, and backend time.
features.buildsOn: observability feature flag (opentelemetry 0.21 dep declared in Cargo.toml but zero cfg(feature="observability") hits in src/ — currently a stub), query-rewriting engine (comment injection), analytics/fingerprinter.rs + histogram.rs, session_migrate.rs application_name tracking, plugin HookContext attributes
features.rationale: Platform teams debugging 'why was this query slow' across app -> proxy -> PG have no joined view; sqlcommenter/OTel-into-pg_stat_activity is exactly what Google/Datadog evangelize, and a proxy is the one place it works with zero app changes for every client. The proxy already parses extended-protocol messages, fingerprints every query, and times backends — emitting a span with attributes (fingerprint, route target, cache hit tier, pool wait, replay events) is wiring, not research. Extracting traceparent from inbound SQL comments and re-emitting as application_name + comment makes pg_stat_activity and slow logs correlate to app traces. Also gives the anomaly detector and audit-chain plugin a trace_id to anchor evidence to — a differentiator no PG pooler ships today.

features.name: Helios Fleet: Control Plane for Many Proxies
features.pitch: One control plane where every proxy registers, pulls signed config/policy/plugin bundles, and reports topology + analytics — manage 200 sidecars like one.
features.buildsOn: src/edge/registry.rs (registration + bounded-mpsc fanout + prune-stale is the exact pattern, already shipped), GET /topology + admin REST v2, Ed25519 trust-root + OCI artefact loader (signed bundle distribution already solved for plugins), k8s operator + Terraform provider (become fleet clients), anomaly event ring buffer
features.rationale: The recommended deployment is a per-pod sidecar (README), which means a real customer runs hundreds of instances — and today each one is configured by hand-edited TOML and polled on its own :9090. Banks standardize on things with a pane of glass (pgcat has none; AWS RDS Proxy's is its console). Crucially the hard primitives already exist in-repo: EdgeRegistry proves the register/heartbeat/fanout model, and the plugin signing + OCI pipeline is reusable verbatim for signed config/policy distribution (no new trust machinery). Phase 1 (aggregation: fleet-wide topology, anomalies, version inventory) is M-sized and already differentiating; phase 2 (push config/plugins, coordinated drains, canary-by-cohort) is the XL tail that locks in enterprise adoption.

features.name: Blue/Green Database Cutover GA (finish the Upgrade Orchestrator)
features.pitch: One API call migrates a live workload PG-12->17 or onto new hardware: replicate, shadow-verify, buffered cutover, replay, retire — with drift metrics and one-command rollback.
features.buildsOn: src/upgrade_orchestrator/mod.rs (full 6-state machine exists; module docs admit every transition 'is a stub that logs and advances state'), shadow_execute row-hash diff, switchover_buffer.rs, failover_replay.rs + transaction_journal.rs, POST /api/replay, chaos API for rehearsal
features.rationale: The README and ecosystem copy already advertise 'zero-downtime PG-12->17 upgrade' as a headline differentiator, but the orchestrator's transitions are stubs — the gap between marketing and code is a credibility risk and the highest-ROI completion in the repo. Every hard component is shipped and tested separately (shadow diffing, write buffering, journal replay, cursor restore); what's missing is the glue: drive logical replication setup, gate Validated on shadow_execute parity over sampled traffic, expose POST /api/upgrade + a UI panel + an UpgradePlan CRD on the operator. For banks, scheduled-downtime major upgrades are a top-3 database pain; a proxy that orchestrates them end-to-end with evidence (parity reports) is a buying trigger, not a feature.

features.name: Connection Storm Protection and Post-Failover Slow-Start
features.pitch: Survive the reconnect avalanche: handshake admission control, a fair client wait-queue with backoff hints, pause/resume per database, and ramped backend reconnects after failover.
features.buildsOn: connection_pool.rs lease management, switchover_buffer.rs (drain/ramp logic), rate_limit/ (token bucket per IP/tenant — query-level today, extend to connection/handshake level), circuit_breaker per-node state, health_checker thresholds
features.rationale: The classic enterprise outage is not steady-state load — it's 10k app pods reconnecting simultaneously after a deploy or failover, melting the new primary (the exact scenario RDS Proxy and pgcat market against). HeliosProxy does 1.2s automatic failover but then lets every client stampede the promoted node; rate limiting exists only at query granularity. Adding (a) a max-concurrent-handshakes gate, (b) a bounded FIFO wait-queue that holds startup until a pool slot frees (instead of erroring), (c) admin PAUSE/RESUME per database (PgBouncer parity, required for runbooks), and (d) token-bucket ramped backend connection establishment post-failover reuses existing primitives and turns the HA story from 'fails over fast' into 'fails over safely under production load' — verifiable with the existing chaos API in a demo.

features.name: Audit-Grade Logging Pipeline + Compliance Evidence Export
features.pitch: Guaranteed-delivery, hash-chained audit stream to WORM/S3/syslog with backpressure semantics, plus one-call signed evidence bundles mapped to SOC 2 / PCI-DSS-10 / HIPAA controls.
features.buildsOn: audit-chain plugin + env.sha256_hex host import (tamper-evident chain exists, but lives in in-proxy plugin KV), anomaly event ring buffer + GET /anomalies, analytics/slow_log.rs, transaction_journal.rs retention machinery, column-mask + residency-router plugins (the controls that need evidencing), admin REST
features.rationale: Banks don't ask 'can you mask the column' — they ask 'prove to the auditor it was masked for the last 90 days'. Today every audit artifact is volatile: the hash chain sits in plugin KV memory, anomalies in a ring buffer, analytics in-process; a restart destroys the evidence. Ship a durable export pipeline (file + fsync, syslog, S3/object-lock, Kafka via reqwest-style sink) with explicit overflow policy (block vs degrade — the 'audit-grade guarantee' knob), anchor chain heads with Ed25519 signatures (key handling already in-tree), and a GET /api/evidence?from=&to= endpoint that emits a signed bundle: chain segment + verification result + active policy snapshot (mask rules, residency map, quotas from plugin KV) + anomaly log. The admin surface itself must also gain authn (src/admin.rs currently has zero auth on any endpoint, including the SQL console — grep shows no Bearer/401 paths), which this feature forces and which is independently a release blocker for regulated deployment.

features.name: SLO-Based Adaptive Routing with Capacity Forecasting
features.pitch: Declare p99/error SLOs per route class; the proxy shifts reads, sheds to cache, and brownouts low-priority traffic to defend the budget — and forecasts when you'll run out of headroom.
features.buildsOn: load_balancer.rs latency strategy, lag/ routing thresholds + RYW, analytics/histogram.rs + statistics.rs (per-fingerprint latency distributions already collected), query-cache serve-stale potential, rate_limit cost estimation, anomaly/ewma.rs (EWMA/z-score code reusable for trend baselines), Prometheus surface
features.rationale: Existing routing reacts to instantaneous signals (lag, latency, breaker state); platform teams operate on SLOs and error budgets — the Google-SRE vocabulary their leadership audits them on. The data plane already computes everything needed: per-fingerprint histograms, replica lag, cache hit ratios, per-tenant cost estimates. Layer on: per-route-class SLO targets in proxy.toml, multi-burn-rate budget tracking (reusing the EWMA machinery in src/anomaly/ewma.rs), and graduated responses — re-weight replicas, serve-stale from L2 cache, then shed analytics-intent queries (intent classification exists in analytics/intent.rs) before OLTP. The same time-series feed a linear-trend capacity forecast ('pool saturation in ~9 days') exposed on /metrics and the admin UI. No PG proxy on the market does SLO-native routing; it converts the analytics module from a dashboard into a control loop.

features.name: Instant Branch Databases (helios branch)
features.pitch: Neon/PlanetScale-style copy-on-write database branches provisioned and routed entirely through the proxy — `helios branch create feature-x` and connect with `?options=branch=feature-x`.
features.buildsOn: src/replay/ + transaction_journal.rs (journal-window hydration already exists via POST /api/replay), src/routing/ + schema-routing (branch-name routing), multi-tenancy per-pool isolation, HeliosDB-Nano native branching in the ecosystem
features.rationale: Branching is the single biggest bottom-up adoption driver in modern DB DX (Neon, PlanetScale), and HeliosProxy is uniquely positioned to deliver it for *vanilla* Postgres: the time-travel replay engine can hydrate a branch from a journal window (the /api/replay endpoint with target_user/target_database overrides already does 80% of this), Postgres template databases give cheap copies, and the routing layer can map a branch name in startup params to a backend. For Nano backends it is nearly free since Nano branches natively. No competitor proxy (PgBouncer, PgCat, Supavisor) offers this.

features.name: Migration Safety Gate (ddl-gate feature)
features.pitch: Lint every DDL statement on the wire, auto-inject lock_timeout/statement_timeout before it runs, and block table-rewrite footguns (CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY, volatile defaults, NOT NULL without validation) with a proper PG error and fix suggestion.
features.buildsOn: src/rewriter/ (rules engine + transformer already rewrites SQL pre-backend), llm-guardrail plugin (precedent for refusing dangerous statements with synthesized ErrorResponse via RouteResult::Block), src/upgrade_orchestrator/ + switchover_buffer.rs for a phase-2 online-DDL orchestration mode
features.rationale: Every team that has taken prod down with an ALTER TABLE wants this; tools like squawk lint files, but only the proxy sees the DDL that actually executes (including ad-hoc psql sessions). The rewriter can inject `SET lock_timeout` as a prepended statement and the Block ABI already synthesizes clean PG errors. Phase 2 reuses the upgrade_orchestrator's settled state machine (stand-up/shadow/validate/cutover/drain) for gh-ost-style online DDL — the module header in src/upgrade_orchestrator/mod.rs explicitly says the integration points are wired. High pull for content marketing ('your proxy refuses your bad migration').

features.name: Query Flight Recorder with Shareable Replay Bundles
features.pitch: `helios record --window 5m` captures fingerprints, params, timings, and plans into a signed portable .tar.gz; anyone replays it against any backend with `helios replay bundle.tgz` and gets a shadow-diff report.
features.buildsOn: transaction_journal.rs (statement-level capture with parameter fidelity), src/replay/ (window replay), src/shadow_execute/ (order-independent row-hash diffing), src/analytics/ (fingerprinter, histograms, slow_log), helios-plugin pack's OCI .tar.gz + Ed25519 format reused for the bundle
features.rationale: Turns 'works on my machine' DB debugging into a shareable artifact — paste a bundle in a GitHub issue or Slack and a teammate reproduces the exact perf regression. All four primitives exist (journal capture, replay, shadow diff, analytics enrichment); the work is composing them behind one CLI verb and serializing to the already-proven signed tarball format from src/plugins/loader.rs. This is the feature that makes devs screenshot HeliosProxy.

features.name: ORM-Native Insights (Prisma/Drizzle/SQLAlchemy/ActiveRecord)
features.pitch: Detect the ORM from application_name and query shape, then surface N+1 and slow-query findings where devs actually live: dev-mode PostgreSQL NOTICE messages that appear inline in the ORM's own log, plus marginalia-comment parsing to pin findings to file:line.
features.buildsOn: src/analytics/patterns.rs (N+1 detection exists but is trapped behind the admin API), fingerprinter.rs + intent.rs, ai-classifier plugin (precedent for application_name keyword parsing), routing/hint_parser.rs (comment parsing infrastructure for marginalia/traceparent tags)
features.rationale: N+1 detection already works (patterns.rs) but no developer sees it — it lives in an admin endpoint ops people own. Injecting an async NOTICE on the wire in dev mode means a Rails or Prisma dev sees 'HeliosProxy: N+1 detected, 47x SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id=$1, from app/models/user.rb:32' in their normal console with zero integration work. Wire-level NOTICE injection is cheap in the hand-rolled backend client (src/backend/). This is the lowest-friction path from 'installed a pooler' to 'this tool made me a better developer' — the conversion moment for bottom-up growth.

features.name: Plugin Registry + One-Command Install
features.pitch: `helios-plugin install column-mask` resolves a signed artefact from a public registry index, verifies it against the trust root, drops it in plugin_dir, and hot-reload picks it up live; `helios-plugin new` scaffolds a plugin from a template.
features.buildsOn: helios-plugin CLI (pack/inspect/verify already exist), src/plugins/loader.rs OCI .tar.gz loader with SHA-256 manifest validation + Ed25519 SignatureVerifier, src/plugins/hot_reload.rs, the 8 first-party plugins as launch catalog
features.rationale: The entire trust pipeline is built — signed artefacts, manifest hashes, trust roots, hot reload — but distribution is still 'clone the plugins repo and cargo build'. A static registry index (JSON over HTTPS, artefacts on GitHub Releases) plus an `install` subcommand is days of work and converts the WASM platform from a feature checkbox into an ecosystem with network effects. Marketplaces are how proxies become platforms (cf. Envoy/Kong); third-party plugins are also free marketing and the moat competitors can't fast-follow.

features.name: SQL-Level Feature Flags (flag-routing)
features.pitch: Declare flags in the admin KV and gate rewriter rules on them per tenant/user/percentage: dual-write to a new schema for 5% of tenants, kill-switch a pathological query pattern in prod without a deploy, A/B a rewritten query.
features.buildsOn: src/rewriter/ (matcher + rules + transformer), plugin host KV (PUT /admin/kv runtime config without restart), multi-tenancy tenant identification, shadow_execute for validating the flagged-on path before ramping
features.rationale: The rewriter is currently static config; making its rules toggleable through the existing live KV surface turns it into LaunchDarkly-for-SQL. The killer workflow — flag on a schema change for 1% of tenants, shadow-diff old vs new results, ramp to 100, then drop the flag — composes three modules that already ship. Strong differentiation story (no other PG proxy has it) and a natural pairing with the Migration Safety Gate for expand/contract migrations.

features.name: Typed Schema Diff + Live Drift Guard (helios schema diff)
features.pitch: Snapshot a typed schema catalog through the proxy, diff between branches/environments, and uniquely cross-reference against live query analytics: 'dropping users.ssn breaks 3 query fingerprints seen 1,200x in the last 24h'.
features.buildsOn: graphql-gateway schema introspection (src/graphql/ already builds a typed catalog from information_schema), schema_routing metadata, src/analytics/fingerprinter.rs query corpus for usage-aware breaking-change detection, branch databases (feature 1) as the natural diff target
features.rationale: Schema diffing exists elsewhere (Atlas, migra) but only HeliosProxy can join the diff against what queries actually hit those columns — the analytics fingerprint store is a live usage index no offline tool has. The introspection code is already written for the GraphQL gateway; this repackages it as a dev-facing CLI verb and a CI-friendly JSON report. Pairs with branching to give a complete 'diff my branch against prod, with blast radius' story.

features.name: helios dev: One-Command Local Stack + TUI Console
features.pitch: `helios dev up` starts the proxy plus an ephemeral Postgres/Nano backend with fixtures/*.sql seeded through the proxy, then drops into a ratatui console streaming live queries, latency histograms, N+1 alerts, topology, and plugin state.
features.buildsOn: Admin REST v2 (all panels are already JSON: /topology, /plugins, /anomalies, /metrics, /sessions), src/analytics/histogram.rs + slow_log.rs, src/skills.rs include_dir! precedent for embedding fixture templates and demo assets in the binary, demos/v0.4.0/_shared compose patterns
features.rationale: The web admin UI targets operators; devs live in terminals. Every JSON endpoint the TUI needs already exists, so this is a pure client built into the existing binary (the clap subcommand structure landed in 0.4.2). The dev-up + fixture-seeding half removes the 'write a docker-compose and init.sql' tax that every one of the 22 demos currently pays manually, and is the on-ramp that makes a first-time user productive in 60 seconds — the metric that decides bottom-up adoption.
